config.title: The Oberbaumbrücke: FAQ
config.subtitle: The history, the border years, and how to walk it today.

items.a: The Oberbaumbrücke is a red-brick double-decker bridge over the Spree that connects the districts of Kreuzberg and Friedrichshain. The U1 and U3 U-Bahn lines run on the upper deck, while the road, bike lane and pedestrian walkways run below. It is one of Berlin’s most recognisable and most photographed landmarks.

items.q: Why does the Oberbaumbrücke look medieval if it opened in 1896?
items.a: The look is deliberate. Architect Otto Stahn finished the bridge in 1896 in a North German brick-Gothic style, and its two 34-metre towers were modelled on an old Brandenburg city gate. So the bridge only looks centuries old; it is really a late 19th-century design dressed up to echo a medieval gate.

items.q: Was the Oberbaumbrücke a border crossing between East and West Berlin?
items.a: Yes. When the Wall went up in 1961 the bridge sat on the border between the American sector (Kreuzberg, in the West) and the Soviet sector (Friedrichshain, in the East). From 21 December 1963 it worked as a pedestrian crossing, and only West Berlin residents were allowed through it.

items.q: When did the Oberbaumbrücke reopen after the Berlin Wall came down?
items.a: The Wehrmacht blew up the middle span in April 1945, and reconstruction began in 1992. The bridge reopened to pedestrians and traffic on 9 November 1994, exactly five years after the Wall fell, with a new steel middle section designed by Santiago Calatrava. The U-Bahn line returned across it in 1995.

items.q: How do I get to the Oberbaumbrücke, and is it free to cross?
items.a: It is free to walk across at any time. From the Kreuzberg side, take the U1 to Schlesisches Tor and walk about four minutes down Oberbaumstraße. From the Friedrichshain side, use Warschauer Straße, served by the U1, U3, S-Bahn and the M10 tram. Crossing on foot takes roughly ten minutes if you stop for photos.

items.q: When is the best time to visit the Oberbaumbrücke and what should I look for?
items.a: Late afternoon into dusk is best: the brick glows and, from nightfall until about 1am, a neon "Stone, Paper, Scissors" light game plays in the arch over the river. Wait for a yellow U-Bahn to cross overhead for the classic shot, and look upstream from the bridge to spot the Molecule Man sculpture standing in the Spree.
